Tag Showing 0 of 100 Conservatism Article Space is not just the future of ‘discovery science’, but a current geopolitical reality The Artemis mission captured the imagination – just as Apollo did over 50 years ago. Peter Franklin’s column last week tried to locate Space on the political spectrum. That is hard to do as in many countries including Britain it enjoys cross-party support. But it is certainly however of great strategic significance. View Conservatism Article Care is needed when regulating young people’s lives – protection not restraint is the key The dramatic shift to much tougher regulation of social media is driven above all by growing evidence of its damage to children. There is a clear correlation between the rise of mental health problems amongst younger people and access to social media. View Conservatism Article Labour’s New Deal for young people has a focus on apprenticeships – a good idea if understood properly My column a fortnight ago looked at the three key Conservative proposals for a New Deal for young people – removing the interest rate on graduate debt, boosting apprenticeships and diverting £5,000 of national insurance into a savings pot. View Conservatism Article Apprenticeships and the ‘New Deal’ for young people One of the strong themes on Conservative Home is the importance of the Conservative Party reaching out to younger voters. So it is great to see the Conservative Party launching its New Deal for Young People which is a bold attempt to plug this gap. Any evidence of the party thinking beyond its core vote of pensioners is to be welcomed. View Conservatism Article The Conservative Party cannot disown its past I had better start with a confession. Yes, I was at the launch of Prosper UK – there were many old friends there. I believe both Andy Street and Ruth Davidson are experienced political leaders and communicators from whom today’s party can learn a lot. David Gauke’s excellent column yesterday set out the case for the initiative very well. View Conservatism Article Is there a better way to pay for higher education? Many areas of British public policy are subject to wild oscillations and turbulence. Funding higher education is much more stable. For the past twenty years all three political parties, faced with difficult decisions about how to pay for it, have opted for broadly the same model. But as we are seeing again now that does not mean the system is accepted let alone actually welcomed. View Conservatism Article It’s the Economy, stupid The Tory dispute with Reform gets spicier every day. But beyond the personal attacks there is a real divide about political priorities. View Science & Technology PDF Regulation and innovation: Promoting growth through regulatory reform Britain’s growth rate has not recovered from the financial crash: indeed, our performance was already weakening from about 2005. Although our performance has been particularly bad, other Western countries also have a growth problem.
